item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding JetPack, some grammar mistakes and site-XX around tge articles. But again, +1 for the effort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall I like it, but there's a few mistakes (like Site-XX), but still a +1 because I like how you handled the incident report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with sindar. Though there could be more, we don't know this. It should be safe as it's easy to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure, but here I was thinking it should be Keter. If another one of these ever were to show up "in the wild," say perhaps on a busy subway platform in the London underground, it could push millions of people to Stage 6 before it's detected. If this somehow wasn't a deliberate attack on the Foundation, but rather just a random special delivery to the wrong address from across the Multiverse, we're basically all doomed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this Euclid? As far as I can see from the description, leave it locked away in its container and nothing will happen. I call this Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disproven for some time now.

item.title: Re: Changing the Nickname
item.content:encoded:
it escalates through a generic sequence of Bad Brain Things that aren't properly characterized, and then there's a shitload of words that don't matter and/or aren't worth caring about.

It's a match made in heaven, really.

Seems unnecessarily mean-spirited, especially that last line.

Anyway, OP, regarding the title: there's likely others that are more "long, hard to say, and the two words don't flow well together" than what you've got. I think you're fine here, title-wise.

item.title: Re: Changing the Nickname
item.content:encoded:
Those are all either cool or interesting, which makes up for being long. "99.7 Cognitohazard FM" isn't really either of those.

Really, the current title is appropriate. It suggests that there will be a radio station with mindfuckery going on, but not anything else. The article itself has radio stations and it escalates through a generic sequence of Bad Brain Things that aren't properly characterized, and then there's a shitload of words that don't matter and/or aren't worth caring about.

It's a match made in heaven, really.

item.title: Re: Changing the Nickname
item.content:encoded:
"99.7 Cognitohazard FM" is too long, hard to say, and the two words don't flow well together. I'd also recommend titling it simply 99.7 or Broadcast or something like that. A more clever name would do also, but 99.7 Cognitohazard FM just ain't gonna cut it.

No love for:

  • SCP-1168 - A Highly Immature and Completely Unofficial Parody
  • SCP-1383 - Partially-Functioning Pataphorical Torment Chamber
  • SCP-1521 - The Most Holy Bank of His Holiness Pope Leo the Tenth, Saint in Waiting
  • SCP-2061 - "Entire Local Family Chokes To Death On Single Calculator"
  • SCP-2139 - Indeterminate object or objects, or possibly a memetic effect of some kind
  • SCP-2462 - I'm so tired of fake friends, drama, liars, being ignored, and getting hurt
  • SCP-2511 - Kappa Population Distribution: More in Mongolia than anywhere else
  • SCP-2637 - A controversial chunk of rock with 196,884-dimensional stakes
  • SCP-2858 - As Though The Wasps Themselves Had Worked It There
  • SCP-2963 - A D-Class, a Sarkicist, and a Yithian walk into a reality bender…

?

item.title: Changing the Nickname
item.content:encoded: Personally, I'd recommend changing the Nickname of the article. "99.7 Cognitohazard FM" is too long, hard to say, and the two words don't flow well together. I'd also recommend titling it simply 99.7 or Broadcast or something like that. A more clever name would do also, but 99.7 Cognitohazard FM just ain't gonna cut it. Gotta say though, the cognitohazard is kinda interesting though. Maybe expand on the individual who appears in the room, and possibly tie it back to an idea that instead of Hallucinating, the individuals are given the ability to see another piece of our reality, and that the headphones are simply a device that the entity is "connected" to and follows around/broadcasts it's own voice through said headphones. (Does anyone else feel awesome saying words like CognitoHazard or Memetic Infohazards?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry you didn't like it. It's only my first attempt at writing an SCP and we all have to start somewhere.

I know some of the ideas could be developed more but I'm planning on developing the article in the future to try and improve on some of the points you have mentioned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This comes across as particularly dull and generic. It's stored in a giant cubic cell for no reason. The tests repeat the information we've learned in the Description.

The anomaly is essentially a bog-standard "insanity and then death" effect, with the twist being the common (and difficult to believe) "someone who knows all about the Foundation is using it as a weapon". The PoI is never made a character or humanised, which might have made this interesting.

The note at the end doesn't tell us anything we haven't already learned (although it also doesn't make sense - SCP-3115/A hasn't been described, so how does the D Class know that the person in the video is /A?). And the note is also too casual - "this guy", "all such 'deliveries'" - which is noticeable throughout the skip.

So much of this is cliché - I don't see the value here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You actually explained it pretty well cake. The "First SCP Curse" is almost like motivation to learn and do even better the second time around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To add to ShadowMagesAlpha's testing list:

  • Are the cognitohazards even words? (I may or may not decide to start a forum thread on this subject)
  • Can repeating those words at the same POINTS in time, but not by the same person have any effects?
  • (Just for laughs) Can it kill 682?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "first SCP curse" is suppose to just be something that people use to vent their emotions about their First SCP failing. It tends to be easier for humans to have a reason rather than feeling just as others did not like their idea or the like. It tends to make people attempt again as they feel it wasn't their mistakes or ideas that caused them to fail, and, of course, makes some learn from what others think of what they have written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do believe that this disproves the "first SCP curse"

Nothing special really. Hell, SCP-1000 was the first SCP written by its author (thedeadlymoose).

That "first SCP" curse was never a thing, just something we tell ourselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just some ideas for testing…

  • Does it affect deaf people? (The victim-to-victim transmition does not, but what about the SCP itself?)
  • How long must a break bebetween uses before the 15 minutesis reached?
  • What happens when each bud is used by a different person?
  • What occurs if used by a non-human?
  • Must the individual understand the damaging words? (Or what if one whom does not understand English use the SCP?)
  • What do the damaging words do when reversed?
  • Can the damaging words be negated by disruption through destructive interference?
  • Can the damaging words by negated by "drowning them out" with a higher decible sound?

…that's all I've got for now. I'll certainly think of more later. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please continue to post if all your writing is this good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If more testing logs is what you are after, the Department of Science will get busy testing! I am planning to write more testing logs for this SCP in the future so check back. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow thanks a lot! I have been a fan of the wiki for years and I have lots of ideas so will be posting many more SCP's in the future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do believe that this disproves the "first SCP curse"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this quite interesting. This was written well enough to get a good range of opinions on this SCP.
"Oh, this is neat! A world-wide variety radio!" … "Change of narator?… that's weird…" … "Migraines and halucinations? That's… unsettling…" … "Oh shit, this is an auditory nuke! DO NOT WANT!"

Good escalation and execution. I'd like to see more testing, but I love testing logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I guess we know how SCP: Containment Breach II will start
Pretty interesting concept, I must say. I also like that you explained how it came into the Foundation's hands and how that is linked to the properties.
